stem: A breastfeeding term newborn boy is reviewed at 48 hours of life for visible jaundice. The mother is blood group O Rh-positive and the baby is group A Rh-positive with a positive direct antiglobulin test. The first total serum bilirubin at 30 hours of life was 280 micromol/L and has risen to 460 micromol/L at 48 hours despite intensive double phototherapy initiated 12 hours ago. He is feeding poorly but alert.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.9 degrees Celsius, HR 152, RR 48, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Alert and responsive, mild hypotonia, normal Moro reflex, no opisthotonus, no high-pitched cry
data_table.Investigations: Total bilirubin 460 micromol/L, conjugated 12 micromol/L, haemoglobin 142 g/L, reticulocytes 6%, DAT positive, weight loss 7% from birth

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue intensive double phototherapy and repeat bilirubin in 6 hours
options.rationale: Continuing phototherapy alone is inappropriate when bilirubin has continued to rise on intensive phototherapy and now sits above the exchange transfusion threshold for haemolytic disease; delay risks kernicterus. (Source: RWH Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Administer a single dose of intravenous immunoglobulin 0.5 g/kg only
options.rationale: IVIG is an adjunct in haemolytic disease when bilirubin rises despite intensive phototherapy, but is given alongside preparation for exchange when bilirubin exceeds the exchange threshold, not as a stand-alone substitute. (Source: RWH Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Arrange double-volume exchange transfusion with cross-matched compatible blood
options.rationale: A bilirubin of 460 micromol/L at 48 hours in a term infant with DAT-positive ABO haemolytic disease exceeds the Australian exchange threshold of around 400 micromol/L for haemolytic disease and mandates double-volume exchange transfusion. (Source: RWH Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Stop breastfeeding for 24 hours and supplement feeds with infant formula
options.rationale: Breastfeeding cessation is not indicated when ongoing haemolysis is the driver; effective hydration is important but does not substitute for exchange transfusion in severe haemolytic disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

options.text: Start oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg daily to induce bilirubin conjugation
options.rationale: Phenobarbitone has a slow onset over days and is not used in acute severe hyperbilirubinaemia; it has historical use in some conjugation disorders but no role in DAT-positive haemolytic disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Neonatal Jaundice)

explanation.summary: Severe hyperbilirubinaemia in a term newborn with positive DAT signals ABO or Rh haemolytic disease. Australian neonatal thresholds for exchange transfusion in term babies with haemolysis are around 400 micromol/L at 48 hours, lower than for non-haemolytic jaundice. Adjuncts include IVIG and ensuring adequate hydration, but when the bilirubin exceeds the exchange threshold the double-volume exchange is non-negotiable to prevent kernicterus. Acute neurological signs (lethargy, hypotonia, poor feeding, opisthotonus, retrocollis, high-pitched cry) lower the exchange threshold further.
key_points: DAT-positive haemolytic disease has lower exchange thresholds than non-haemolytic jaundice
Exchange transfusion is indicated when bilirubin exceeds the haemolysis-specific threshold despite intensive phototherapy
Acute bilirubin encephalopathy signs are an immediate exchange indication regardless of level
explanation.pearls: In Australian neonatal jaundice charts the exchange transfusion threshold for haemolytic disease at 48 hours in a term baby is around 400 micromol/L; for non-haemolytic disease it is around 450 micromol/L.

stem: An 8-month-old previously well boy was admitted 4 days ago with high fever and a clean-catch urine growing more than 100,000 colony-forming units per millilitre of Escherichia coli sensitive to gentamicin and cephalexin. He responded promptly to intravenous gentamicin and is afebrile on oral cephalexin to complete a 10-day course. His renal tract ultrasound performed yesterday is normal with no hydronephrosis. He is discharged tomorrow and feeding normally.

data_table.Examination: Afebrile, well, normal external genitalia, retractile foreskin, no abdominal mass, growth on the 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Urine culture E. coli more than 100,000 CFU/mL, CRP peak 110 mg/L, creatinine 28 micromol/L, renal ultrasound normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: No further imaging; routine review by the general practitioner at 6 weeks
options.rationale: A first febrile UTI in an infant under 24 months still warrants further evaluation when the ultrasound is normal; Australian and NICE pathways recommend additional functional or anatomical imaging based on age and atypia. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram before discharge while still inpatient
options.rationale: Routine MCUG is not first-line after a typical first febrile UTI that responded within 48 hours; it is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI, or abnormal ultrasound findings. (Source: NICE NG224)

options.text: Magnetic resonance urography under sedation in the next month
options.rationale: MR urography is a problem-solving tool for complex anatomy or duplex systems; it is not part of the routine post-UTI imaging pathway in infancy. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to assess for renal cortical scarring
options.rationale: A late DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months after a first febrile UTI in an infant under 3 years is the Australian and NICE-recommended strategy to detect renal cortical scarring after acute pyelonephritis resolves. (Source: RCH CPG: UTI)

options.text: Long-term low-dose trimethoprim prophylaxis without further imaging
options.rationale: Antibiotic prophylaxis after a first uncomplicated febrile UTI does not replace imaging and is itself controversial; it is considered after recurrent UTIs or in confirmed high-grade reflux. (Source: NICE NG224)

explanation.summary: After a first febrile UTI in an infant under 24 months that responds well to treatment, Australian pathways recommend renal tract ultrasound within 6 weeks (or sooner if atypical) plus a DMSA scan at 4 to 6 months to detect cortical scarring. MCUG is reserved for atypical features (non-E. coli, slow response, septicaemia, raised creatinine, poor stream) or for those with abnormal ultrasound or DMSA. Atypical UTI shortens these timelines and lowers the threshold for MCUG.
key_points: First febrile UTI under 24 months: ultrasound plus delayed DMSA at 4 to 6 months
MCUG is reserved for atypical or recurrent UTI
Antibiotic prophylaxis is not routine after a single uncomplicated UTI
explanation.pearls: DMSA is deferred to 4 to 6 months after a febrile UTI because acute inflammation produces transient defects that may mimic scarring; scanning earlier risks false-positive findings.

stem: A 15-year-old rugby player presents to the local clinic 24 hours after being tackled during a match. He had a 20-second loss of consciousness, brief retrograde amnesia and a headache. He has had no further headache since the morning of presentation, no vomiting, and his neurological examination today is normal. His parents ask when he can return to contact training. He had a previous concussion 8 months ago that resolved in 1 week.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 72, RR 14, BP 118/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Glasgow Coma Scale 15, normal cranial nerves, normal coordination, normal tandem gait, no scalp haematoma

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate advice today?

options.text: Immediate return to non-contact training tomorrow if he stays asymptomatic
options.rationale: Adolescents require at least a 24- to 48-hour rest period of relative cognitive and physical rest at Stage 1 before initiating graded return-to-play steps. (Source: AIS Concussion in Sport)

options.text: Permanent retirement from contact sport because of repeat concussion
options.rationale: A second concussion within 12 months requires careful staged return and longer symptom-free interval, but a single recurrence does not mandate retirement; that decision follows multiple or persistent post-concussion syndrome. (Source: AIS Concussion in Sport)

options.text: Strict cognitive and physical rest for 4 weeks then resume contact
options.rationale: Prolonged complete rest delays recovery; current evidence supports relative rest for 24 to 48 hours then gradual symptom-limited activity progression. (Source: Concussion in Sport Group 2022)

options.text: Computed tomography of the head before any return to activity
options.rationale: CT head is indicated in concussion only when red flags such as focal deficit, repeated vomiting, prolonged GCS depression, seizure or skull fracture risk are present; routine CT is not part of return-to-play assessment. (Source: AIS Concussion in Sport)

options.text: Begin a graded return-to-sport protocol with at least 24 hours per stage
options.rationale: Current Australian Institute of Sport and Concussion in Sport Group guidance for under-19 athletes is a stepwise return with at least 24 hours per stage and a minimum 14-day symptom-free interval before full contact. (Source: AIS Concussion in Sport)

explanation.summary: The 2022 Concussion in Sport Group consensus and AIS guidance for under-19 athletes use a 6-stage graded return-to-sport protocol with at least 24 hours per stage, beginning with symptom-limited activity after 24 to 48 hours of relative rest. A symptom-free interval of at least 14 days off medication is required before full contact in adolescents. Recurrence within 12 months prompts a more conservative pace and specialist follow-up. CT head is reserved for red flags rather than routine return-to-play assessment.
key_points: Under-19s: 6-stage graded return with at least 24 hours per stage
Minimum 14-day symptom-free interval before full contact in adolescents
CT head is reserved for red flags, not for routine return-to-play clearance
explanation.pearls: AIS guidance mandates that an adolescent who sustains a second concussion within 12 months should not return to contact sport for at least 4 weeks symptom-free, longer than the typical 14-day adolescent minimum.

stem: A 28-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 weeks is admitted in spontaneous labour with contractions every 4 minutes. Her vaginal-rectal GBS swab at 36 weeks was positive. She has no allergies. Her previous baby was healthy and discharged at 48 hours with no neonatal sepsis screen. Membranes are intact and the cervix is 4 cm dilated.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 88, BP 118/72 mmHg, contractions every 4 minutes
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, vertex at station 0, membranes intact, fetal heart rate 140 with normal variability

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis?

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g load then 1.8 g every 4 hours until birth
options.rationale: Australian RANZCOG and Therapeutic Guidelines recommend intravenous benzylpenicillin 3 g loading dose then 1.8 g every 4 hours during labour for GBS-positive women without penicillin allergy. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g three times daily commencing now and continuing until birth
options.rationale: Oral antibiotics achieve inadequate intrapartum tissue concentrations to prevent vertical GBS transmission; intravenous administration is required. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Intravenous clindamycin 600 mg every 8 hours until birth in absence of allergy
options.rationale: Clindamycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with documented isolate susceptibility; this patient has no allergy so penicillin is first-line. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 20 mg/kg every 12 hours intrapartum until birth occurs
options.rationale: Vancomycin is reserved for severe penicillin allergy with clindamycin-resistant GBS; it is not first-line in a non-allergic woman. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

options.text: Intravenous cefazolin 2 g load then 1 g every 8 hours intrapartum until birth
options.rationale: Cefazolin is an alternative for non-severe penicillin allergy; in a non-allergic woman benzylpenicillin remains the standard agent because of its narrower spectrum. (Source: RANZCOG GBS)

explanation.summary: Intrapartum antibiotic prophylaxis prevents early-onset neonatal GBS disease. The Australian RANZCOG recommendation is benzylpenicillin 3 g IV at labour onset then 1.8 g IV every 4 hours until delivery, given for at least 4 hours before birth for optimal effect. Alternatives by allergy severity are cefazolin (non-severe), clindamycin with susceptibility (severe), or vancomycin (severe with clindamycin resistance). Indications include GBS-positive swab, GBS bacteriuria in this pregnancy, previous GBS-affected infant, or risk-based criteria where universal screening is not used.
key_points: Benzylpenicillin 3 g IV load then 1.8 g IV every 4 hours is first-line in non-allergic women
Allergy ladder: cefazolin (non-severe) -> clindamycin if susceptible (severe) -> vancomycin
Aim for at least 4 hours of antibiotic before birth for maximal prevention
explanation.pearls: At least 4 hours of intrapartum penicillin before birth reduces vertical GBS transmission from approximately 50% in unprotected labour to less than 5%.

stem: A 24-year-old woman with PCOS (Rotterdam criteria, hyperandrogenism plus oligomenorrhoea plus polycystic ovaries) attends for review. She has had no menstrual bleeding for 9 months. She is not sexually active, not contemplating pregnancy in the next 2 years, and finds daily medication burdensome. Her BMI is 31 kg/m2. She has tried metformin in the past without benefit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, weight 84 kg, height 165 cm
data_table.Examination: Mild acne and hirsutism on the chin, no acanthosis nigricans, no galactorrhoea
data_table.Investigations: Pregnancy test negative, TSH 1.8 mU/L, prolactin 320 mU/L, fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate strategy to protect the endometrium?

options.text: Cyclical medroxyprogesterone 10 mg orally daily for 12 days every 8 weeks
options.rationale: Cyclical progestogens give adequate endometrial protection but rely on daily oral dosing, which the patient has identified as burdensome and at risk of non-adherence. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023)

options.text: Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system inserted for 5 years' protection
options.rationale: The LNG-IUS provides effective endometrial protection in PCOS-related anovulatory amenorrhoea and avoids the daily-medication burden the patient describes, with a 5-year duration. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023)

options.text: Continued metformin 1 g twice daily as monotherapy with lifestyle support
options.rationale: Metformin improves ovulatory function modestly but does not reliably restore menses or provide endometrial protection in established oligomenorrhoea. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive ethinyloestradiol 30 mcg with levonorgestrel daily
options.rationale: A COCP is first-line in many PCOS women, but requires daily adherence which the patient has flagged as a barrier, and her BMI of 31 raises VTE risk. (Source: International PCOS Guideline 2023)

options.text: Hysterectomy with ovarian conservation as a definitive long-term solution
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is disproportionate for a 24-year-old with PCOS-related amenorrhoea, removes future fertility options, and ignores effective reversible strategies. (Source: RANZCOG PCOS)

explanation.summary: Anovulatory amenorrhoea in PCOS produces unopposed oestrogen exposure of the endometrium and a higher long-term endometrial hyperplasia and cancer risk. Endometrial protection options include LNG-IUS (5 years, no daily burden), cyclical or continuous progestogens, or a combined hormonal contraceptive. The International PCOS Guideline 2023 supports patient-centred choice based on contraceptive needs, adherence preferences, BMI and metabolic risk. Lifestyle modification remains a first-line non-pharmacological strategy.
key_points: PCOS amenorrhoea requires endometrial protection; bleeding at least every 3 months is the goal
LNG-IUS, COCP, cyclical or continuous progestogen are all valid options
Choice is patient-centred: contraceptive needs, adherence, BMI, metabolic risk
explanation.pearls: The International PCOS Guideline 2023 specifically endorses the LNG-IUS as a low-adherence-burden first-line option for endometrial protection in women who do not want pregnancy and find daily medication difficult.
